title: Add ordered string-list editing
description:
## Why

Ratconfig currently treats allowed `string_list` multi-choice editing like a set. That works for unordered feature toggles, but it cannot represent host settings where the order of selected ids is meaningful. The implementation should add ordered-list controls without changing the existing unordered multi-choice contract by default.

## Evidence

- `src/editor.rs:539`: `edit_mode_for_field` maps any enum-backed `string_list` field to `ConfigUiEditMode::MultiChoice`.
- `src/editor.rs:723`: `multi_choice_status_value` reports enabled count and highlighted allowed value, not selected-item order.
- `src/editor.rs:738`: `toggled_string_list_input` toggles the highlighted allowed value in or out of the parsed list.
- `src/editor.rs:753`: toggled values are always passed through `ordered_string_list_values`.
- `src/editor.rs:758`: `ordered_string_list_values` rebuilds selected values in `allowed_values` order through a set, so arbitrary user order is lost.
- `src/editor.rs:787`: `is_enum_string_list_field` is only `kind == "string_list" && !allowed_values.is_empty()`, so there is no separate ordered-list behavior flag today.

## Scope

In scope:
- `src/editor.rs` edit behavior/mode changes for an explicitly ordered string-list field.
- `src/model.rs` only for a narrow behavior flag/variant if needed.
- `src/render.rs` only if the UI needs to display ordered selected values or reorder hints.
- Focused tests for preserving order, toggling, and reordering.

Out of scope:
- Do not change default `MultiChoice` set semantics for existing fields.
- Do not encode host/product ids, widget names, labels, file paths, apply behavior, or runtime refresh commands.
- Do not add drag/drop, mouse support, or a new UI framework.
- Do not make unordered scalar enums or boolean choice editing depend on this feature.

## Implementation Notes

1. Depend on the string-list helper bead so hosts can opt into ordered behavior through a small public API instead of relying on `kind` alone.
2. Add an explicit opt-in for ordered string-list editing, for example a `ConfigUiEditBehavior` variant or similarly small flag. Existing `string_list` fields should keep current allowed-values ordering.
3. Preserve parsed config order for selected values. Adding a new allowed value may append it to the selected list; removing a value should keep the order of the remaining values.
4. Add minimal reorder commands/handlers only where the existing editor architecture already routes choice actions. Avoid broad keymap or rendering rewrites.
5. Make invalid inputs fail through the same allowed-value validation path as current string-list parsing.

## Verification

- `cargo fmt --all -- --check` should exit 0.
- `cargo test` should exit 0, including tests that an ordered string-list field preserves `["b", "a"]` rather than normalizing it to allowed-values order, and that the default unordered multi-choice behavior remains unchanged.

## Stop Conditions

- Stop if adding ordered controls requires a broad editor event-loop redesign rather than a narrow behavior extension.
- Stop if this starts changing existing unordered multi-choice behavior without an explicit opt-in.
- Stop if the implementation requires host-specific product concepts.
- Stop if verification fails twice for the same reason after a reasonable fix attempt.

## Maintenance Notes

- This is intentionally separate from the helper/API bead. If a host only needs config-order validation and JSON/TOML persistence, it should be able to use that bead without adopting ordered-list UI controls.
- Rejected alternative: reusing `allowed_values` order as the only ordering model. That keeps code small but fails settings where selected order is observable behavior.
notes: Fresh-eyes correction: Ctrl+j was replaced with uppercase J and Ctrl+k with uppercase K for ordered string-list reordering because Ctrl+J is commonly encoded as line feed/Enter by terminals. The public behavior is J/K reorder; no crate or packaging impact.
close_reason: Completed: added ConfigUiEditBehavior::OrderedStringList as the explicit opt-in for order-preserving string-list multiselect editing. Ordered fields keep parsed config order when toggling, append newly enabled values, remove disabled values without reordering survivors, and support uppercase J/K to move the highlighted enabled id down/up. Default string-list multiselect still canonicalizes to allowed_values order. Rendering shows ordered selected values and the J/K reorder hint for ordered fields only. Fresh-eyes fix replaced Ctrl+j/k because Ctrl+J is commonly encoded as line feed/Enter in terminals. No new crates or packaging impact. Verified with focused ordered/default string-list tests, crossterm Shift+J conversion coverage, cargo fmt --all -- --check, git diff --check, cargo test, cargo clippy -- -D warnings, cargo test --features crossterm-runner, cargo clippy --features crossterm-runner -- -D warnings, cargo test --no-default-features, and cargo clippy --no-default-features -- -D warnings.

title: Render selected config source metadata
description:
## Why

The current header assumes one active config path for the whole UI. Multi-file hosts need the selected tab/source to show the relevant path, owner, existence, and write mode so users can tell which managed file they are editing. Without this, a UI with separate file-backed tabs can edit the right file internally but still display misleading global metadata.

## Evidence

- `src/model.rs:10`: `ConfigUiModel` has one `active_config_path`, `active_config_exists`, `config_owner`, and `config_read_only` for the whole UI
- `src/render.rs:75`: `render_header` reads only the model-level owner/write state/path
- `src/render.rs:82`: missing-file text is derived from the single `active_config_path`
- `src/render.rs:187`: tabs are rendered from bare `String` labels, with no source metadata
- `src/model.rs:39`: row filtering is tab-label based, while only the hardcoded `advanced` tab gets non-field operational rows

## Scope

In scope:
- Add project-agnostic source/tab metadata that can describe a host-owned config document: id, display label, path, exists/missing state, owner, and read-only/write mode
- Render header metadata from the selected tab/source when present, with the existing single-model path as a compatibility fallback
- Keep the `advanced` operational tab behavior intact unless a smaller replacement falls out naturally from the new model
- Add focused render/model tests for two file-backed tabs with different paths and write modes
- Update affected README examples after the API shape is settled

Out of scope:
- Do not load, create, patch, migrate, or atomically write any config file inside Ratconfig
- Do not include Yazelix-specific path names, Mars/Zellij wording, or Zellij sidecar policy in Ratconfig docs or model types
- Do not add status rows for product-specific native config surfaces in this bead
- Do not change text adapter semantics for JSONC or TOML

## Implementation Notes

1. Build on `ratconfig-cvf` so field source identity and UI source metadata share one id vocabulary
2. Prefer a small neutral type such as `ConfigUiSource`/`ConfigUiTabSource`; avoid a broad document-management abstraction
3. Keep old single-file construction reasonably ergonomic, either through fallback model fields or a tiny helper
4. Ensure narrow terminal widths still truncate the selected path cleanly and keep owner/mode/diagnostic metadata readable
5. Keep tests focused on the contract: selected source metadata, fallback behavior, and no regressions to advanced rows

## Verification

- `cargo fmt --all -- --check` should exit 0
- `cargo test` should exit 0, including model/render tests for selected source metadata
- `cargo test --features crossterm-runner` should exit 0

## Stop Conditions

- Stop if the implementation starts making Ratconfig responsible for host persistence or config discovery
- Stop if the API requires product-specific source kinds instead of neutral host-supplied metadata
- Stop if the header/layout change makes narrow-width rendering less stable than the current global metadata line

## Maintenance Notes

Dependency decision: add no crates. This should be model/rendering plumbing over existing `PathBuf`, owner enum, and render helpers. Rejected alternative: keep one global `active_config_path` and ask hosts to put the selected file path in field descriptions; that hides core context in the details pane and makes the header misleading for multi-file editors.
notes: User clarification: selected-source metadata is UI context only. This bead should not introduce config contracts, versioning, migrations, joined state, or source-file creation policy. Hosts can use it for simple render/edit tabs over separate files; any future contract/migration layer must be an explicit opt-in outside this UI metadata work.
close_reason: Completed: added ConfigUiSource metadata and selected_config_source lookup, exported the source API, rendered selected tab source label/path/owner/write mode in the header with legacy active_config_path fallback, kept advanced/unmapped tabs on fallback behavior, documented host-owned source metadata, and added focused model/render tests. Dependency decision unchanged: no new crates; this is UI metadata plumbing over existing PathBuf and owner/read-only fields. Verified with cargo fmt --all -- --check, cargo test, cargo clippy -- -D warnings, cargo test --features crossterm-runner, cargo clippy --features crossterm-runner -- -D warnings, cargo test --no-default-features, cargo clippy --no-default-features -- -D warnings, and git diff --check.

title: Keep long choice-picker focus visible
description:
## Why

A choice editor can move its logical focus beyond the visible details viewport while the rendered option list remains clipped from the top. The footer continues to identify the off-screen candidate, but the `>` cursor disappears, so users cannot see what Enter will commit. This is reproduced by editing Yazelix's Zellij theme field, whose host-supplied choice inventory is taller than the terminal.

## Evidence

- `src/editor.rs:38-45`: `ConfigUiEditState::choice_index` is the logical focus owner for choice and multichoice edits.
- `src/editor.rs:795-812`: `move_choice_edit` advances and wraps that index independently of viewport height.
- `src/render.rs:615-636`: the details pane renders one wrapped `Paragraph` without a scroll offset or choice-aware viewport.
- `src/render.rs:1125-1203`: single-choice and multichoice details append every option from index zero, so terminal clipping hides later focused rows.
- Reproduction at release-candidate commit `361f02d`: open a choice field with more options than fit in the details pane, move Down past the last visible option, and observe that the list appears frozen while the edit status names an off-screen candidate.

## User-visible Contract

While editing a single-choice or multichoice field, the focused `>` option is always visible. The field title and selected/enabled summary remain pinned. Moving beyond the last visible option minimally advances the options viewport; moving upward does the inverse. Existing wraparound navigation brings the wrapped first or last option into view. The committed `(x)` or enabled `[x]` state remains visually distinct from focus, and save, toggle, reorder, and cancel semantics do not change. Resizing or replacing/reordering a compatible model recomputes the window so the current focus stays visible. When all options fit, rendering remains unchanged. Show a restrained upper/lower overflow cue when choices are clipped.

## Scope

In scope:
- Ratconfig-owned choice and multichoice rendering in `src/render.rs`.
- The smallest private viewport projection needed to keep `ConfigUiEditState::choice_index` visible.
- One strong small-terminal buffer test covering downward following, upward/wrap following, pinned context, committed-versus-focused markers, and resize behavior.
- Current controls/help documentation only if the visible contract requires an update.

Out of scope:
- Zellij, Yazelix, or host-specific theme knowledge.
- Scrolling arbitrary prose, diagnostics, or host-supplied custom detail callbacks.
- Changing the settings-list viewport.
- New PageUp/PageDown/Home/End key behavior, mouse scrolling, or a public scrolling API.
- Changing choice order, wraparound, Enter/Space, ordered-multichoice reorder, or Escape behavior.

## Implementation Notes

1. Keep `choice_index` as the single logical cursor owner; do not add a second host-visible selection or persistent public scroll field.
2. Derive the visible option window from the details content height after reserving pinned title/summary rows and any overflow cues.
3. Share the windowing rule between single-choice and multichoice editors rather than duplicating offset logic.
4. Prefer a focused choice renderer or private helper over a general scrolling framework. Preserve `draw_config_ui_with_details` callback behavior.
5. Handle extremely short panes without panic; show the pinned context and an honest overflow cue if no option row fits.

## Verification

- `cargo fmt --all -- --check` exits 0.
- `cargo test` exits 0.
- A Ratatui `TestBackend` regression with more choices than visible rows proves that every Down/Up transition leaves the `>` row in the buffer, headers remain pinned, and wrapped/resized focus is visible.
- `cargo test --no-default-features` exits 0.
- `cargo test --features crossterm-runner` exits 0.

## Stop Conditions

- Stop if the fix requires host-specific choice metadata or a second selection owner.
- Stop and split only with user approval if correct behavior requires a new public renderer or input API.
- Do not turn this focused editor bug into general details-pane scrolling.

## Maintenance Notes

Effort: S. Risk: LOW. Confidence: HIGH.

This blocks the unfinished 6.0 publication bead because it is a confirmed dogfood failure in the release candidate's generic choice interaction.

Rejected alternatives: scrolling the entire details paragraph, which would hide field context; centering on every movement, which would make the list jump unnecessarily; storing a renderer-only offset in the public edit model; and truncating the host's valid choice inventory.
notes: Post-closure fresh-eyes passes found and fixed the physical viewport edge cases behind this bug. The renderer now measures Ratatui's actual wrapped height, truncates styled picker rows only when physical content clips, keeps the focus cursor visible even when the details content is one cell wide, keeps the overflow cue on the last actually visible context row when no option row fits, and uses accurate upper/lower/combined cues. Inline cues are separated from summaries and values instead of producing strings such as 'down morezero'. The regression now resizes TestBackend itself (Terminal::resize had been a false-positive because the backend restored its old size) and proves every downward/upward move, both wraps, 72x14 resize, 72x10 total height, a direct one-row viewport, a genuine one-option-row ordered multichoice at 72x11, long labels at 40x14, and the cursor at 7x14. The Crossterm runner selects the exact renderer through one private setup/loop owner and is one net line larger than its original form while preserving cleanup and errors. Public render_details and custom callbacks remain complete and unwindowed. Rejected disabling wrapping globally, approximating rendered height, windowing host callbacks, persistent scroll state, and general prose scrolling because they weaken output or broaden scope. Formatting, 119 default tests, 82 no-default tests, 124 Crossterm-feature tests, strict all-feature Clippy, rustdoc with warnings denied, and diff checks pass.
close_reason: Implemented height-derived choice viewport with pinned context, overflow cues, resize/wrap following, and regression coverage

title: Add string-list choice field helper
description:
## Why

Host applications that expose TOML/JSON config arrays currently have to hand-assemble the low-level Ratconfig field pieces for a selectable string list. Ratconfig already owns the generic model/editor/patch semantics, but there is no compact public helper that says "this path is a list of allowed string ids". A small helper would let hosts keep product-specific ids and text in the host while deleting custom glue code.

## Evidence

- `src/lib.rs:1`: Ratconfig's public boundary is reusable config editor primitives, with applications owning loading, validation, persistence, and apply behavior.
- `src/model.rs:168`: `ConfigUiFieldRowSpec` already carries `kind`, current/default JSON values, `allowed_values`, and `edit_behavior` for manually built rows.
- `src/model.rs:186`: `build_config_ui_field` renders current/default/edit values and copies `allowed_values` into `ConfigUiField`.
- `src/model.rs:226`: `ConfigUiEditBehavior::FriendlyStringList` already exists for friendlier list text input.
- `src/model.rs:275`: `ConfigUiContractField` already stores `kind`, `default_value`, `validation`, and `allowed_values`.
- `src/model.rs:307`: `config_contract_fields_from_toml` already reads contract fields, including `allowed_values` and `default`.
- `src/editor.rs:572`: `parse_edit_input` already routes `string_list` fields through string-list parsers.
- `src/editor.rs:659`: `parse_string_list_values` already validates JSON arrays contain only strings and checks allowed values.
- `src/toml_adapter.rs:99`: `set_toml_value_text` already patches a JSON value into TOML, so array persistence is not host-specific.

## Scope

In scope:
- `src/model.rs` public model/helper API for allowed string-list fields.
- `src/editor.rs` only if a tiny exported validation helper should reuse existing parser/allowed-value behavior.
- `src/lib.rs` exports for any new public helper.
- Focused tests in existing model/editor/toml test areas.

Out of scope:
- No Yazelix-specific widget names, defaults, labels, command names, runtime refresh policy, or Zellij/Home Manager concepts.
- No new config file IO or apply policy in Ratconfig.
- No new dependency unless a concrete implementation need appears; this should be achievable with existing serde/toml_json plumbing.
- Do not redesign the existing `ConfigUiFieldRowSpec` contract or current scalar choice behavior.

## Implementation Notes

1. Add the smallest public helper that removes host boilerplate for a field whose value is an ordered `Vec<String>` constrained by allowed string ids. Prefer one function or one compact spec type over a new module hierarchy.
2. The helper should build the existing `ConfigUiField`/`ConfigUiFieldRowSpec` shape with `kind = "string_list"`, supplied `allowed_values`, current/default JSON arrays, and the chosen edit behavior.
3. Add a public validation/extraction helper only if the builder alone does not let hosts validate TOML-derived values without reconstructing a `ConfigUiField`. It should preserve input order, reject non-strings, reject unknown ids with a clear allowed-values error, and leave product-specific wording to the host.
4. Reuse existing `render_json_value`, `render_json_edit_value`, and TOML patch behavior instead of adding adapters.
5. Keep the API intentionally narrow; hosts pass ids/defaults/labels, Ratconfig supplies only generic field construction and validation.

## Verification

- `cargo fmt --all -- --check` should exit 0.
- `cargo test` should exit 0, including focused coverage for the new helper preserving order and rejecting invalid string-list values.

## Stop Conditions

- Stop if the current `ConfigUiFieldRowSpec` or `parse_string_list_values` code has drifted enough that a tiny helper would duplicate substantial editor logic.
- Stop if the implementation starts encoding host-specific ids, display strings, file paths, or apply behavior.
- Stop if the helper requires a broad public API redesign; create a decision bead instead.
- Stop if verification fails twice for the same reason after a reasonable fix attempt.

## Maintenance Notes

- This bead intentionally does not solve reordering controls in the TUI. It should preserve config order for validation/extraction and keep existing editor behavior unless the ordered-list UI bead is also implemented.
- Rejected alternative: moving a Yazelix-specific widgets adapter into Ratconfig. That would make Ratconfig own product policy and violates the repo boundary.
notes: Dependency decision before implementation: add no production or dev crates. Build in-house using existing ConfigUiFieldRowSpec, serde_json::Value, render_json_value/render_json_edit_value, and existing string-list editor validation semantics. Rejected alternatives: broad UI/form/helper crates, host-specific adapters, or new parsing dependencies; this is small model/API plumbing. Packaging impact: no dependency, feature, rust-version, or runtime closure changes.
close_reason: Completed: added ConfigUiStringListChoiceSpec and build_string_list_choice_field so hosts can build allowed string-list choice fields from ordered Vec<String> values without hand-assembling JSON row specs or kind metadata. Added string_list_values_from_json for host/TOML-derived JSON extraction that preserves order, rejects non-arrays/non-strings, and checks allowed ids. Reused that validation from editor parsing, preserving existing scalar/string-list behavior while adding an order-preservation regression. No new crates or feature changes. Verified with cargo fmt --all -- --check, cargo test string_list_choice, cargo test string_list_values_from_json, cargo test edit_parser_uses_field_type_and_allowed_values, cargo test, cargo clippy -- -D warnings, cargo test --features crossterm-runner, cargo clippy --features crossterm-runner -- -D warnings, cargo test --no-default-features, cargo clippy --no-default-features -- -D warnings, and git diff --check.

title: Pretty-print structured Ratconfig detail values
description:
## Why

Ratconfig renders structured TOML values as compact JSON-like strings in the details pane. Long objects and arrays wrap as dense walls of text, and when an effective value equals its default the same large payload is printed twice. The left list may remain a truncated preview, but the details pane should be the complete, readable inspection surface.

## Evidence

- Runtime dogfooding report on 2026-07-14: Yazi's `opener` object wraps across several opaque lines in details, and identical `current` and `default` objects are duplicated.
- `src/render.rs:466-488` wraps the final detail lines as ordinary paragraph text without structured-value layout.
- `src/render.rs:692-700` renders `current` and `default` unconditionally through the same one-line helper.
- `src/render.rs:1228-1232` concatenates a fixed-width label and an opaque string value into one line.
- `src/model.rs:794-810` stores compact rendered current/default TOML values, including structured tables and arrays.
- `src/model.rs:817-834` reuses those compact strings both for list cells and field details, so the renderer currently has no distinct expanded representation.

## User-Visible Contract

Scalar current/default values remain compact. Structured objects and arrays render in the details pane as complete, indented, multiline values with no truncation. Long nested strings wrap without destroying the visible structure. If current and default are identical, the default line says `same as current` instead of repeating the payload. Non-JSON TOML values such as infinities and NaN remain complete and inspectable through a safe fallback.

## Scope

In scope:
- Add a generic structured-value detail representation in Ratconfig for objects and arrays.
- Pretty-print current and default structures with stable indentation and readable wrapping.
- Deduplicate identical effective/default payloads while preserving the distinction between explicit, defaulted, and unset state.
- Keep scalars and ordinary metadata in the current compact key/value form.
- Preserve complete TOML special values that cannot be represented by strict JSON.
- Add focused renderer/model tests for nested objects, arrays, long strings, identical defaults, differing defaults, and TOML infinity/NaN fallback.
- Publish the Ratconfig change; Yazelix owns its downstream dependency pin, CHANGELOG, LOC scorecard, and integration checks.

Out of scope:
- Do not change list-column widths or list preview truncation; that is the adaptive-columns bead.
- Do not add JSON/TOML editing, syntax highlighting, a copy mode, scrolling, or a detail-only pane mode.
- Do not render the entire source file or replace the existing `e` source-file action.
- Do not introduce Yazi-specific labels, semantic summaries, or paths into Ratconfig.
- Do not include the separate contrast work.

## Implementation Notes

1. Keep compact list previews and expanded detail rendering as two presentations of one field value.
2. Prefer carrying or deriving structured data at the generic Ratconfig model/builder boundary rather than blindly treating every rendered string as JSON.
3. If safe structured data is unavailable for a host-built field, preserve the existing complete wrapped string instead of guessing.
4. Render structured labels on their own line followed by indented content; keep scalar labels and values on one line.
5. When `current_value == default_value` and a real default exists, render the current value once and use `default  same as current`.
6. Rust dependency decision: reuse Ratconfig's existing `serde_json`/`toml` stack and Ratatui text primitives. Add no crate.

## Verification

- In Ratconfig, `cargo fmt --all -- --check` should exit 0.
- In Ratconfig, `cargo test` should exit 0.
- Focused tests should prove nested objects and arrays produce indented multiline detail lines, scalar details stay compact, identical defaults are not duplicated, differing defaults remain visible, and `[inf, -inf, nan]` remains complete.
- Detail output should contain every key/value from the structured input and no ellipsis introduced by Ratconfig.
- Existing edit, choice, multichoice, file-action, diagnostic, and host-supplied rich-detail tests should pass.
- After publishing and locking Ratconfig in Yazelix, `nix flake check` and `nix build .#yazelix --no-link --print-build-logs` should succeed.
- `nix profile add --refresh /home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix --profile <tmp>` should install the updated package.
- Manual dogfooding should inspect Yazi `flavor`, `opener`, arrays, long command strings, and a scalar setting in the normal transparent config popup.

## Stop Conditions

- Stop if the only approach reparses every display string as strict JSON and loses valid TOML values; add a bounded generic representation or preserve the raw fallback.
- Stop if deduplication hides whether a value is explicit versus inherited; state must remain visible.
- Stop if implementation requires host-specific Yazi/TOML policy in Ratconfig.
- Stop if a usable result requires new navigation or scrolling semantics; bring a separate interaction decision back to the maintainer.
- Stop if the implementation begins changing columns or contrast; those have separate beads.

## Maintenance Notes

Owner: Ratconfig's generic field model/detail renderer. Yazelix remains a consumer and lock owner.

Effort: M. Risk: MEDIUM because the model must preserve complete TOML values while improving generic rendering. Confidence: HIGH in the UX defect and MEDIUM/HIGH in the smallest representation.

Rejected alternatives: leaving opaque wrapped JSON, count-only summaries, flattening every object into dotted paths, rendering the whole source file, adding a detail-focus mode in the first slice, and strict-JSON-only reparsing.

title: Add optional crossterm runner for ConfigUiApp
description:
## Why

Ratconfig owns `ConfigUiApp`, `ConfigUiKey`, `ConfigUiIntent`, and `draw_config_ui`, but every crossterm host currently has to write its own raw-mode alternate-screen loop and key mapping before it can use those primitives. This is reusable terminal UI mechanics, not host policy.

## Evidence

- `README.md:13-17`: Ratconfig owns the generic model, edit state, controls, and Ratatui rendering.
- `README.md:90`: Hosts currently build a model, then use editor/rendering helpers inside their own terminal event loop.
- `src/editor.rs:41-72`: Ratconfig already defines the generic key and intent vocabulary.
- `src/render.rs:49-57`: Ratconfig already owns `draw_config_ui` and `draw_config_ui_with_details`.
- Sibling adopter evidence: `../yazelix-next/crates/yzn-config/src/main.rs:60-132` currently duplicates the crossterm terminal session, draw loop, event read, release filtering, key conversion, and intent dispatch around Ratconfig.

## Scope

In scope:
- Add an optional crossterm-backed runner module or helper, gated behind an explicit feature.
- Keep the runner generic over host save/apply behavior, likely by returning intents to callbacks or accepting small host callbacks for `SetField` and `UnsetField`.
- Keep `draw_config_ui_with_details` support so hosts with richer details do not lose functionality.
- Add focused tests for key conversion/dispatch where possible without requiring an interactive TTY.
- Update `README.md` with a minimal runner example.

Out of scope:
- Do not move host schema, validation, file IO, atomic writes, config paths, or runtime apply policy into Ratconfig.
- Do not make crossterm a required dependency for non-TUI/text-patching users.
- Do not hardcode Yazelix command names, config paths, or notices.

## Implementation Notes

1. Record the Rust dependency decision before coding: crossterm should be optional, behind a feature such as `crossterm-runner`, instead of entering the default text-patching surface by accident.
2. Extract a small public key-conversion helper first if that removes most host duplication without committing to a large runner API.
3. If adding a full runner, keep the API callback-oriented: Ratconfig drives terminal lifecycle, rendering, and key/event dispatch; host callbacks perform validation, writes, model reload, and notices.
4. Preserve existing lower-level APIs so hosts can still own their event loop when needed.
5. Add tests around non-interactive pieces and keep manual TTY behavior documented rather than pretending CI can prove terminal rendering.

## Verification

- `cargo fmt --all -- --check` should exit 0.
- `cargo test` should exit 0 and include coverage for any new key conversion or runner state helper.
- If a new feature is added, run `cargo test --no-default-features` and `cargo test --features crossterm-runner` or the final feature name.

## Stop Conditions

- Stop if the API starts requiring hosts to surrender validation, file writes, or apply policy.
- Stop if crossterm must become a default dependency for non-UI consumers.
- Stop if interactive terminal behavior cannot be separated from testable key/intent logic.

## Maintenance Notes

This should remain an optional convenience layer. The core Ratconfig contract stays: Ratconfig owns reusable editor mechanics; hosts own config meaning and persistence.
notes: Dependency decision before implementation: add crossterm only as an optional production dependency behind a non-default crossterm-runner feature. Keep existing ratconfig core and default ui feature free of crossterm. Reuse ratatui's crossterm backend by enabling ratatui/crossterm only for that feature. Build in-house: small key-event conversion and callback-oriented runner glue. Rejected alternatives: making crossterm default, moving host validation/file IO/apply policy into ratconfig, or adding a broader terminal framework. Packaging impact: consumers that do not enable crossterm-runner do not pull the crossterm event-loop surface.
close_reason: Completed: added optional crossterm-runner feature with key/event conversion helpers, callback-oriented run_config_ui/run_config_ui_with_details helpers, non-interactive conversion and dispatch tests, and README usage docs. Verified with cargo fmt --all -- --check, git diff --check, cargo test, cargo test --no-default-features, cargo test --features crossterm-runner, cargo clippy -- -D warnings, and cargo clippy --features crossterm-runner -- -D warnings.

title: Establish Ratconfig versioning policy
description:
## Why

Ratconfig is still versioned as `0.1.0`, but it already has a meaningful host-facing surface: public Rust exports, optional feature flags, JSONC/TOML patch behavior, config contract migration semantics, renderer/editor intent behavior, and documented host integration expectations. Before downstream hosts rely on it more heavily, the repository needs a durable release policy.

The proposed direction is SemVer. Calling the current state `1.0.0` is reasonable only if the maintainers are ready to treat the existing public API and documented behavior as stable. If that commitment is too early, keep the crate on `0.x` but document the exact rule for when it can become `1.0.0`.

## Evidence

- `Cargo.toml` currently declares `version = "0.1.0"` and `rust-version = "1.94"`.
- `src/lib.rs` publicly exports the contract, editor, model, renderer, JSONC, TOML, patch, migration, and optional crossterm runner surfaces.
- `README.md` documents Ratconfig as a reusable crate and describes host-facing behavior for model fields, TOML document rendering, file actions, external text editing, crossterm runner integration, patching, and config contracts.
- Main Yazelix consumes this crate through a pinned git dependency, so versioning policy needs to make integration risk obvious even before crates.io publishing.

## Scope

In scope:
- Define the Ratconfig release/versioning policy in `README.md` or a dedicated docs file.
- Decide whether to set the current crate state to `1.0.0` now or explicitly defer `1.0.0` until named stability criteria are met.
- Use SemVer terminology for release severity instead of a single vague "user-facing contract changed" rule.
- Define Ratconfig's public contract to include public Rust API, feature flags, MSRV, documented host integration behavior, editor intents, renderer/model semantics, JSONC/TOML patch semantics, and config contract migration semantics.
- Add concrete examples for patch, minor, and major bumps.
- Add a short release checklist covering tests, docs, tag/version updates, and downstream Yazelix pinned-git consumption.

Out of scope:
- Do not add Yazelix-specific release policy to Ratconfig.
- Do not publish to crates.io unless that is explicitly chosen in a separate release task.
- Do not promise compatibility for undocumented internals or private modules.
- Do not tie Ratconfig crate SemVer directly to host config contract version numbers; those are different version domains.

## Recommended Policy

- Patch: bug fixes, docs, internal refactors, test changes, and behavior-preserving cleanup.
- Minor: additive public API, additive documented behavior, new optional feature flags, or new helpers that preserve existing host integrations.
- Major: breaking public Rust API changes, changed model/editor/intent/rendering semantics that hosts can observe, changed JSONC/TOML patch semantics, changed config contract migration semantics, removed feature flags, or an MSRV bump if the project chooses to treat MSRV as compatibility-sensitive.
- `1.0.0`: use it only when maintainers are ready to protect the existing public surface under the major-bump rule.

## Acceptance Criteria

- A documented policy exists in the repo and distinguishes crate SemVer from host config contract versions.
- The policy explicitly defines the Ratconfig user-facing/public contract.
- The policy says whether the current state becomes `1.0.0` or why it stays `0.x`.
- Examples classify at least one patch, one minor, and one major change.
- A release checklist names the local verification commands and downstream pinned-git consideration.
- `cargo fmt --all -- --check` and `cargo test` pass if docs or metadata are edited.

## Maintenance Notes

Dependency decision before implementation: add no production or dev crates. This is a documentation and release-policy decision. Packaging impact depends on whether the implementation updates `Cargo.toml` to `1.0.0` now or records why that bump is deferred.
close_reason: Completed: established Ratconfig's stable crate contract at 1.0.0, updated Cargo.toml/Cargo.lock and the README dependency example, and documented SemVer release policy for public Rust API, feature flags, MSRV, model/editor/rendering semantics, JSONC/TOML patching, migration/config contract behavior, host integration responsibilities, release examples, and release checklist. Clarified that crate SemVer is separate from host ConfigContract versions and from downstream pinned-git consumption. Verified with cargo metadata --locked --no-deps --format-version 1, cargo fmt --all -- --check, cargo test, cargo test --no-default-features, cargo test --features crossterm-runner, git diff --check, and jq -s empty .beads/issues.jsonl.

title: Centralize dotted JSON path traversal
description:
## Why

The current worktree has an isomorphic simplification that removes repeated dotted JSON object traversal from JSONC, model, and TOML adapter code. This should be tracked before committing so the change has the same planning and verification record as the surrounding simplification work.

## Evidence

- `src/jsonc.rs:105`: `get_json_path` is a public wrapper over dotted JSON path traversal
- `src/model.rs:536`: model-level `get_json_path` exposes the same traversal for config model helpers
- `src/toml_adapter.rs:117`: `get_toml_path` traverses the JSON value converted from TOML using the same dotted object-key semantics
- `src/patch.rs:11-38`: the shared patch module already owns dotted path parsing and overlap checks, making it the narrowest existing home for shared dotted-path helpers
- Current candidate diff: `src/jsonc.rs`, `src/model.rs`, `src/patch.rs`, and `src/toml_adapter.rs` net to about `14 insertions(+), 26 deletions(-)` while preserving public wrapper function names

## Scope

In scope:
- Keep existing public paths and names: `jsonc::get_json_path`, crate-root `get_json_path` via `model`, and `toml_adapter::get_toml_path`
- Centralize only the duplicated traversal mechanics in `src/patch.rs`
- Keep `dotted_paths_overlap` behavior for invalid paths and strict-prefix checks unchanged
- Preserve the existing focused default-completion tests and broad adapter tests

Out of scope:
- Do not rename public helper functions
- Do not expose a new public helper from `patch` unless a downstream public API need is proven
- Do not collapse `TomlMigrationOutcome` into `MigrationOutcome`; that changes observable nominal type/debug identity even if field access still compiles
- Do not change config path syntax, bracket rejection, trimming, or JSON/TOML parse behavior

## Implementation Notes

1. Use the current worktree diff as the candidate implementation, but reread it rather than applying blindly
2. Keep public wrappers as one-line delegations to an internal shared helper
3. Keep path overlap semantics equivalent: invalid paths still return `false`, and only strict parent/child prefixes overlap
4. Run formatting after edits because import ordering changes

## Verification

- `cargo fmt --all -- --check` should exit 0
- `cargo test defaults_text` should exit 0 and continue to cover JSONC and TOML default completion
- `cargo test` should exit 0
- `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` should exit 0
- `cargo test --no-default-features` should exit 0
- `cargo clippy --no-default-features -- -D warnings` should exit 0
- `git diff --check` should exit 0

## Stop Conditions

- Stop if keeping the public wrappers makes the change net larger or harder to read
- Stop if any path syntax or error behavior changes
- Stop if a downstream caller would need to import a new symbol
- Stop if the refactor starts touching contract semantics, schema validation, file IO, or host apply policy

## Maintenance Notes

Dependency decision before implementation: add no new production or dev crates. Build in-house with existing `serde_json::Value` and `patch` helpers. Rejected alternatives: public re-export aliases for all callers, because that can change API shape; aliasing `TomlMigrationOutcome` to `MigrationOutcome`, because that changes nominal public type identity. Packaging impact: no dependency or runtime closure growth.
close_reason: Completed: centralized duplicated dotted JSON object traversal in patch::get_dotted_json_path while preserving the public jsonc::get_json_path, crate-root get_json_path, and toml_adapter::get_toml_path wrappers. dotted_paths_overlap still rejects invalid paths and only treats strict parent/child prefixes as overlapping. Verified with cargo fmt --all -- --check, git diff --check, cargo test defaults_text, cargo test, cargo clippy -- -D warnings, cargo test --no-default-features, and cargo clippy --no-default-features -- -D warnings.

title: Make Ratconfig field columns width-aware
description:
## Why

Ratconfig's default three-column field list reserves 18 cells for `takes effect`, 44 for `setting`, and only 28 for `value`. In the normal half-screen config popup this leaves a large empty gap after short setting labels while structured values and long paths are truncated much earlier than necessary. The list should remain a compact comparison surface, but it should use the width it actually has.

## Evidence

- Runtime dogfooding report on 2026-07-14: the Yazi tab shows a conspicuous gap before `value`, while flavor, opener, plugin, and native-file values truncate despite unused horizontal space.
- `src/render.rs:11-13` hard-codes the default field widths to 18, 44, and 28.
- `src/render.rs:316-338` gives the list half the terminal width and passes that rectangle into `render_list`.
- `src/render.rs:338-375` knows the list's exact inner width, but headers and rows are built without using it.
- `src/render.rs:613-634` pads every setting to 44 cells and truncates every value at 28.
- `crates/yzx-config/src/model.rs:147-159` supplies a custom table only for Keys, so the Yazi tab correctly exercises Ratconfig's default field layout.

## User-Visible Contract

The default `takes effect | setting | value` list stays column-aligned across a tab, avoids visible padding far beyond the tab's actual labels, and gives `value` all useful remaining width. Values may still end in an ellipsis because the list is a preview, but unused setting padding must not force premature truncation. Layout remains readable without overflow at representative wide, medium, and narrow popup widths.

## Scope

In scope:
- Change Ratconfig's default field-list geometry in `src/render.rs` to resolve widths from the available list width and tab content.
- Keep column positions stable while moving selection and while entering or clearing search; avoid distracting width jitter.
- Give the value column the remaining usable width after bounded status and setting columns.
- Preserve explicit host-supplied `ConfigUiListTable` behavior and Advanced status-table behavior.
- Add focused renderer contracts for width allocation, alignment, truncation, and narrow boundaries.
- Publish the Ratconfig change; Yazelix owns its downstream dependency pin, CHANGELOG, LOC scorecard, and integration checks.

Out of scope:
- Do not remove or abbreviate the `takes effect` column.
- Do not add horizontal scrolling, resizable panes, a detail-only mode, or a six-column TOML table to Yazi.
- Do not change the 50/50 body split in this slice.
- Do not change colors or include the separate light-mode contrast bead.
- Do not add Yazelix/Yazi-specific layout rules to Ratconfig.

## Implementation Notes

1. Add one small pure width resolver whose inputs are available inner width and the default field rows for the selected tab.
2. Use explicit minimums and caps for status and setting; allocate the remainder to value. Derive stable tab widths rather than selection-dependent widths.
3. Pass the resolved widths through both header and row rendering so alignment has one owner.
4. Preserve section headings, file-action rows, invalid-state styling, and custom host table rendering.
5. Rust dependency decision: use Ratatui and standard-library width information already present. Add no crate.

## Verification

- In Ratconfig, `cargo fmt --all -- --check` should exit 0.
- In Ratconfig, `cargo test` should exit 0.
- Focused renderer tests should prove a screenshot-equivalent width gives the value column materially more than 28 cells when setting labels are short, headers and rows share exact boundaries, narrow layouts do not overflow, and search/selection do not shift column starts.
- Existing custom table and Advanced status-table tests should remain unchanged or pass with equivalent behavior.
- After publishing and locking Ratconfig in Yazelix, `nix flake check` and `nix build .#yazelix --no-link --print-build-logs` should succeed.
- `nix profile add --refresh /home/lucca/pjs/yazelix-dir/yazelix --profile <tmp>` should install the updated package.
- Manual dogfooding should inspect ordinary scalar tabs, the Yazi tab's structured previews and file paths, and a narrow popup.

## Stop Conditions

- Stop if the proposal changes explicit host-supplied table widths; keep that as a separate contract.
- Stop if width allocation requires storing terminal geometry in the model; keep geometry in Ratconfig's renderer.
- Stop if a narrow layout can only be made safe by silently deleting a semantic column; bring that responsive-boundary decision back to the maintainer.
- Stop if the implementation begins changing detail rendering or contrast; those have separate beads.

## Maintenance Notes

Owner: Ratconfig's generic default field-list renderer. Yazelix remains a consumer and lock owner.

Effort: M. Risk: MEDIUM because this changes geometry across every default Ratconfig tab. Confidence: HIGH.

Rejected alternatives: one new fixed width tuple, horizontal scrolling, removing `takes effect`, changing the pane split, enabling the six-column TOML table, and adding Yazi-specific rendering.
notes:
Transferred from yazelix-5vl-ratconfig-adaptive-columns-y69 on 2026-07-14 because default field-list geometry is owned by Ratconfig. The Yazelix issue is closed as a cross-repo duplicate. Ratconfig owns implementation and publication; Yazelix owns its downstream dependency pin and product integration.

Implementation completed on 2026-07-14: the default field renderer resolves one bounded takes-effect/setting/value geometry from the available inner width and every field/file-action row in the selected tab. Status and setting grow only to tab-content needs (caps 18/44 including spacing); value receives every remaining cell. Header and rows share the resolved widths, searches and selection cannot cause jitter, and zero/narrow areas stay bounded. Explicit host tables and the Advanced status layout retain their existing rendering paths. Fixed-width row_line_for_model compatibility remains unchanged. Added focused allocation, alignment, search-stability, and narrow-boundary contracts plus README guidance. Dependency decision: no new crates or feature changes.

Verification: cargo fmt --all -- --check; cargo test (93); cargo test --no-default-features (62); cargo test --all-features (97); cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings; cargo clippy --no-default-features -- -D warnings; focused renderer tests; and git diff --check all pass. Publication, Yazelix pin/integration checks, and live consumer dogfooding remain.

Fresh-eyes and simplification review on 2026-07-15: found and fixed a terminal-cell bug in the published adaptive layout. The resolver counted Unicode scalar values and used Rust string padding, so CJK labels could under-allocate columns and joined emoji could be split. The private adaptive-field path now uses Ratatui terminal-width and grapheme APIs; CJK/emoji coverage and all width assertions use terminal cells. Inlined the one-use list_layout_for_width wrapper at the render owner and combined overlapping boundary regressions. Rechecked zero through wide allocations, ASCII equivalence, exact first/second-column padding, value non-overflow, search stability, custom host tables, Advanced status rows, and the sibling Yazelix consumer. No further defect or proof-backed simplification remained. No dependency, public API, ASCII output, custom-table, or Advanced-layout behavior changed. Rejected a direct unicode-truncate dependency, changing public truncate/fixed_label semantics, inlining field_column_widths into render_list, and bypassing visible_rows_for_tab_search because those options add dependency/API blast radius or duplicate row ownership. Full Ratconfig and Yazelix consumer verification pass.

The default field list resolves stable per-tab status/setting widths from all rows, assigns remaining space to value, and stays bounded at zero/narrow widths; header and rows share one geometry. Custom host tables and Advanced status tables remain unchanged. Ratconfig fmt, 93 default tests, 62 no-default tests, 97 all-feature tests, and strict Clippy passed. Yazelix passed 44 flake checks, all-system output evaluation, package build, temporary profile install, and live 80/160-column Yazi dogfooding; wide structured previews gained about 25 cells and search did not shift column starts.

title: Open text field edits in a host editor
description:
## Why

Text field editing is currently append/backspace-only, so fixing a character in the middle of a value requires deleting everything after it and typing it again. The chosen UX direction is to reuse the existing host-owned editor flow instead of adding a native cursor/line-editor implementation: when editing a text field, the user can open the current value in the host editor, save, and return to Ratconfig with the edit buffer updated.

## Evidence

- `src/editor.rs:29`: `ConfigUiEditState` stores only `field_index`, `input`, `mode`, and `choice_index`; it has no cursor or external-edit state.
- `src/editor.rs:288`: text edit mode saves on `Enter` and cancels on `Esc`.
- `src/editor.rs:291`: `Backspace` only pops the end of the string.
- `src/editor.rs:292`: `Ctrl+u` only clears the whole string.
- `src/editor.rs:293`: typed characters only append to the end of the string.
- `src/render.rs:856`: text edit rendering shows the whole input with a trailing underscore cursor, which matches append-only behavior.
- `src/render.rs:906`: text edit controls currently expose only `Enter save`, `Esc cancel`, and `Ctrl+u clear`.
- `README.md:202`: file action rows already use a host-owned editor/open boundary through `ConfigUiIntent::OpenFile`.
- `README.md:214`: host integrations already handle Ratconfig intents and own file IO/editor policy.

## Scope

In scope:
- Add a project-agnostic Ratconfig intent for editing the current text edit buffer in a host editor.
- Trigger it only while `ConfigUiEditMode::Text` is active. A likely first key is `Ctrl+e`, but choose the final key during implementation based on terminal/key conflicts.
- Include enough intent data for the host to launch an editor and return a replacement buffer: field index, source id, path, current input, and an optional display/extension hint if needed.
- Add a public method on `ConfigUiApp` for the host to apply the returned external-editor text to the active edit buffer without saving it automatically.
- Preserve the existing save/cancel contract: after returning from the editor, `Enter` still emits `SetField`, `Esc` still cancels, and normal parsing/validation still happens through `parse_edit_input`.
- Update text edit footer copy to expose the external editor key.
- Add focused tests for the intent payload and for applying returned editor text to the staged buffer.
- Update README host integration docs to show how a host can handle the new intent.

Out of scope:
- Do not implement native cursor movement, delete-at-cursor, or line editing in this slice.
- Do not make Ratconfig depend on Helix, `$EDITOR`, temp files, process spawning, or terminal suspend/restore policy.
- Do not change choice or multi-choice editing.
- Do not save the field automatically when the editor exits; returning from the editor only updates the staged edit buffer.
- Do not move Yazelix-specific editor selection, Helix wrapper behavior, config paths, or runtime policy into Ratconfig.
- Do not change file action rows or advanced-tab source-file editing except where docs need to distinguish the two host-owned editor flows.

## Implementation Notes

1. Keep Ratconfig as the reusable owner: it emits an intent and updates staged text when the host gives text back. The host owns launching Helix or any other editor.
2. Prefer a narrow new intent over overloading `OpenFile`; file actions open a real config file, while this feature edits a temporary value buffer.
3. The host implementation can mirror the advanced-tab editor lifecycle: write the current buffer to a temporary file, leave/suspend the Ratatui screen as needed, run Helix, read the file back, then call the new Ratconfig apply method.
4. If the crossterm runner cannot safely support host editor launching from inside the event loop, keep the runner generic and document that the host intent handler owns any terminal teardown/re-entry required before spawning the editor.
5. Treat a canceled/failed host editor as no buffer change plus a visible notice owned by the host or `ConfigUiApp` helper; do not silently save partial data.

## Verification

- `cargo fmt --all -- --check` should exit 0.
- `cargo test` should exit 0 with tests proving the new text-edit intent and returned-buffer application behavior.
- `cargo test --features crossterm-runner` should exit 0 if key conversion or runner behavior changes.
- `git diff --check` should exit 0.
- Downstream Yazelix/Yazelix Next integration should be able to handle the new intent by opening Helix and returning to Ratconfig without Ratconfig gaining Yazelix-specific code.

## Stop Conditions

- Stop if implementing this requires Ratconfig to spawn Helix or know Yazelix runtime paths.
- Stop if terminal suspend/restore cannot be kept host-owned without breaking the optional crossterm runner contract.
- Stop if the chosen key conflicts with existing text input behavior in a way that would make normal field editing worse; record the conflict and choose a different chord before coding.
- Stop if returned editor text needs multiline semantics that conflict with the current single-line edit footer; ask whether multiline field editing is part of the product scope.

## Maintenance Notes

Rejected alternative for this slice: native cursor movement in `ConfigUiEditState`. It is smaller in pure Ratconfig LOC but less aligned with the Yazelix/Helix-centered UX the maintainer wants. Rejected alternative: make Ratconfig launch Helix directly. Ratconfig must remain product-agnostic, and host applications already own editor launch, validation, file IO, and terminal lifecycle.
notes:
Dependency decision before implementation: add no production or dev crates. Build in-house with a narrow ConfigUiIntent variant, an app method that updates the active staged text buffer, existing key handling, and existing README examples. Rejected alternatives: native cursor/line editing, overloading OpenFile, or spawning an editor from Ratconfig. Packaging impact: no dependency, feature, rust-version, or runtime closure changes.

Fresh-eyes correction: clarified in README that optional crossterm runner callbacks run while the runner terminal session is active, so hosts launching a full-screen editor own terminal restore/re-entry policy or should use lower-level editor/render APIs and own the event loop.

Updated text edit controls to show Ctrl+e editor, documented the host-owned editor/temp-file/newline policy in README, and covered crossterm Ctrl+e conversion. Ratconfig still does not spawn editors, create temp files, save automatically, or encode Yazelix-specific policy. title: Simplify duplicated contract join plumbing
description:
## Why

The JSONC and TOML contract adapters duplicate the same join, join-from-version, reconcile, and joined-state writing algorithm. The duplicated code differs only in adapter-specific read/apply/write callbacks and error mapping, which makes future contract behavior changes easy to drift between formats.

## Scope

In scope:
- Keep all public JSONC/TOML contract function names, return types, and error variants unchanged
- Move only the shared contract join/reconcile state machine into private helpers in src/contract/mod.rs
- Leave adapter-specific parsing, migration application, patching, and error mapping in the JSONC/TOML modules
- Preserve existing ordering, state mutation reporting, NotJoined, ContractMismatch, ManualRequired, and patch/migration error behavior

Out of scope:
- No public API rename or compatibility shim
- No dependency changes
- No migration semantic changes
- No host file IO/apply policy in Ratconfig

## Maintenance Notes

Dependency decision before implementation: add no production or dev crates. Build in-house with private helper functions over existing ContractApplyOutcome, ContractJoinOutcome, ContractState, and PatchMutation. Rejected alternatives: merging JSONC/TOML public adapter modules or changing nominal patch outcome types, because those are public API changes rather than isomorphic simplification.

## Verification

- cargo test contract
- cargo fmt --all -- --check
- cargo test
- cargo test --no-default-features
- cargo test --features crossterm-runner
- cargo clippy -- -D warnings
- cargo clippy --no-default-features -- -D warnings
- cargo clippy --features crossterm-runner -- -D warnings
- git diff --check
close_reason: Rejected after proof: extracting duplicated JSONC/TOML contract join/reconcile plumbing preserved behavior and passed cargo test contract, but the Rust adapter scaffolding added more code and indirection than it removed. Reverted the contract-code changes per simplification stop condition; keep the duplicate contract adapter bodies until a smaller seam appears.
